title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream flowing through our rohe that holds cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the mangakōtukutuku (blue duck/whio), a taonga species once abundant here. Students will connect with local kaumātua who share pūrākau about the awa's historical significance as a food source and spiritual pathway.
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data (pH, nitrate levels, turbidity) from three sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using scientific equipment.
Compare current water quality data with historical data provided by Hawke's Bay Regional Council to identify trends in environmental health.
Explain how human activities in our catchment (agriculture, urban runoff) impact the stream ecosystem using cause-effect diagrams.
Co-design a kaitiakitanga action plan with local kaumātua to address one identified issue affecting the awa.

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa
activities.description: Students meet with local Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua at the streamside to hear pūrākau (traditional stories) about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, learning its cultural history, original name, and significance. They create an annotated map labeling cultural sites and historical features.

activities.title: Taiao Tohu - Environmental Indicators
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students measure and record scientific parameters (pH, nitrates, dissolved oxygen, turbidity) at three locations: upstream near native bush, mid-stream near college grounds, and downstream near agricultural land. They also conduct a macroinvertebrate survey to assess ecological health.

activities.title: He Aha te Take? - Cause and Effect Analysis
activities.description: Students analyze their collected data to identify patterns and anomalies. Working in small groups, they create detailed cause-effect diagrams that link land use practices (observable in our catchment) to specific water quality measurements, incorporating both scientific and mātauranga Māori perspectives.

activities.title: Mahi Ngātahi - Collaborative Planning
activities.description: Students present findings to a panel including kaumātua and local council representatives. They workshop potential solutions grounded in both science and tikanga Māori, focusing on practical, community-based approaches to improving stream health.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council historical water quality data sets for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Local Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hone Smith and Aroha Brown for cultural guidance
LaMotte Water Monitoring Kits or equivalent water testing equipment
NIWA's Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit (SHMAK) resources

tasks: Scientific report presenting water quality data with graphs and analysis
Cause-effect diagram linking land use to water quality impacts
Oral presentation of kaitiakitanga action plan to community panel

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in science, Participating and contributing
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic relationships with other earth systems
